meta.title: M6 E63/E64 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW M6 E63/E64 engine — the S85 5.0 V10. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the rod bearing risk explained. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW M6 E63/E64 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned S85 V10 engine typically costs £8,000–£15,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE]. Fitting adds £1,170–£1,430. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Is the M6 S85 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S85 is a motorsport‑derived masterpiece — BMW's only production V10, producing 507 hp with an 8,250 rpm redline. However, rod bearing wear is the defining weakness: premature wear can lead to catastrophic bottom‑end failure. Throttle actuator failure is also common. A well‑maintained S85 with documented rod bearing replacement can be reliable, but ownership costs are significant. Budget for preventative rod bearing work (£1,200–£2,500) on any car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW M6 E63/E64?

acceptedAnswer.text: Rod bearing wear — the S85's single biggest risk, which can cause catastrophic engine failure. Throttle actuator failure — common on higher‑mileage cars. VANOS wear — leading to reduced low‑end torque and rattling. SMG III transmission issues — hydraulic pump failure and clutch wear. High oil consumption — normal but should be monitored.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW M6 E63/E64 worth buying?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M6 is a modern classic — the S85 V10 is one of the greatest naturally aspirated engines ever built. However, it is not a cheap car to own. Rod bearing replacement, throttle actuator repairs, and SMG maintenance are significant costs. A well‑maintained example with documented rod bearing history is a worthwhile investment. A neglected example can become a financial disaster.

mainEntity.name: Which M6 variant should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No variant to avoid — the S85 is the only engine. However, avoid any car without documented rod bearing history. The SMG III transmission is also a consideration: some owners prefer the 6‑speed manual, which is rarer and more robust. The convertible (E64) adds 200 kg and slightly different weight distribution compared to the coupé (E63).

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my M6 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Key warning signs: rod bearing failure — knocking noise, oil pressure warning, metallic debris in oil; throttle actuator failure — reduced power, engine warning light, limp mode; VANOS wear — rattling on start‑up, reduced low‑end torque. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing an M6 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £40,000 M6, a £8,000–£15,000 S85 recon is 20–38% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £15,000 example, the same recon is 53–100% — borderline to unviable. Our 70% BMW threshold is the line: if the lowest viable replacement cost exceeds 70% of the vehicle's current market value, evaluate scrap value first.

mainEntity.name: How long do M6 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The S85 can exceed 100,000–150,000 miles with proper maintenance. The rod bearings are the limiting factor — addressed proactively with upgraded bearings, the engine is robust. Regular oil changes, cooling system maintenance, and throttle actuator inspections are essential. Many owners replace rod bearings at 40k–60k miles as preventative maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the E63 and E64?

acceptedAnswer.text: The E63 (2005–2010) is the coupé — the fixed‑roof version with a carbon fibre roof. The E64 (2005–2010) is the convertible — with a folding soft top, adding weight and slightly different weight distribution. Both share the same S85 V10 engine and SMG III or manual gearbox options. Costs and driving dynamics differ — see the E64 generation page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW M6 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine number is stamped on the block on the front of the engine near the timing cover. The S85B50 is the only engine fitted to the E63/E64 M6 — all variants share the same engine code. Tell us your variant and approximate registration date and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW M6 E63/E64 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: The only engine ever fitted to the V10‑powered M6 — the legendary S85B50 5.0‑litre naturally aspirated V10, shared with the E60 M5. Real UK costs and honest reliability data for BMW's high‑revving masterpiece. Backed by real UK enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW M6 E63/E64 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: The single engine fitted to the V10‑generation M6 (E63 coupé, E64 convertible) — from the May 2005 launch to the September 2010 end of production.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: Ratings are [PROVISIONAL] — no dedicated Engine Page exists yet for the S85B50. The S85 is BMW's only production V10 — a high‑revving naturally aspirated masterpiece with an 8,250 rpm redline, producing 507 hp at 7,750 rpm and 520 Nm of torque at 6,100 rpm. While the engine is a motorsport‑derived icon, it suffers from well‑documented reliability issues: rod bearing wear is the single biggest risk, often leading to catastrophic bottom‑end failure. Throttle actuator failure is also common. A well‑maintained S85 with documented rod bearing replacement can be reliable, but ownership costs are significant.

overview.intro: The M6 E63/E64 is the high‑performance version of the second‑generation BMW 6 Series, produced from May 2005 to September 2010. Designed under the direction of Chris Bangle, the M6 succeeded the 16‑year hiatus of the M6 nameplate. It was offered in coupé (E63) and convertible (E64) body styles. Under the bonnet, the M6 uses the S85B50 — BMW's only production V10 — a 5.0‑litre naturally aspirated engine shared with the E60 M5. The engine produces 507 hp at 7,750 rpm and 520 Nm of torque at 6,100 rpm with an 8,250 rpm redline. Transmission is a 7‑speed SMG III automated manual, with a 6‑speed manual available in some markets.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW E63/E64 6 Series (front‑engine, rear‑wheel drive) • Predecessor: BMW M6 (E24, 1983–1989) • Successor: BMW M6 (F06/F12/F13) • Sibling body styles in this generation: E63 (coupé), E64 (convertible).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The M6 E63/E64 generates consistent UK enquiries [PATTERN DATA], concentrated on well‑maintained examples as these V10‑powered grand tourers enter collector and enthusiast territory.

items.quote: The S85B50 5.0‑litre V10 — BMW's only production V10 and a motorsport‑derived masterpiece — produces 507 hp at 7,750 rpm with an 8,250 rpm redline. It is one of the greatest naturally aspirated engines ever built, but reliability is a significant concern. Rod bearing wear is the defining weakness — premature wear can lead to catastrophic bottom‑end failure. Throttle actuator failure is also common. The engine is fundamentally robust for a high‑performance V10, but owners must budget for preventative rod bearing replacement (typically £1,200–£2,500). Buyers should prioritise a well‑maintained example with documented rod bearing history.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Rod bearing wear, throttle actuators, VANOS
rows.ownershipVerdict: Iconic & Expensive — budget for rod bearings

economicsRule.title: The M6 E63/E64 rule
economicsRule.text: A £8,000–£15,000 S85 recon against a £15,000–£60,000 M6 sits at 13–100% of the car's value. On a sub‑£20,000 M6, that approaches our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well‑kept £40,000+ example, the same recon drops to a routine 20–40% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first. The rod bearing is the single biggest risk — budget for preventative work (£1,200–£2,500) on any car.

problems.title: S85 Rod Bearing Wear
problems.affectedModels: M6 E63/E64 all variants (2005–2010) with S85B50 engine.
problems.rootCause: The S85's rod bearings are the defining weakness of this otherwise magnificent engine. Premature bearing wear is well‑documented and can lead to catastrophic bottom‑end failure if not addressed. The issue is exacerbated by the engine's high‑revving nature and the tight bearing clearances from the factory.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying an M6 with no documented rod bearing history, budget for replacement immediately — £1,200–£2,500 now is far cheaper than the £8,000–£15,000 replacement that follows a catastrophic failure. Many owners replace bearings with extra‑clearance aftermarket units.

cta.label: Compare S85 engine prices for your M6 →

problems.title: S85 Throttle Actuator Failure
problems.affectedModels: M6 E63/E64 all variants (2005–2010) with S85B50 engine.
problems.rootCause: The S85 uses electronic throttle actuators to control the 10 individual throttle bodies. These actuators are a common failure point, leading to reduced power, engine warning lights, and in some cases, limp mode.
problems.recommendation: If your M6 shows reduced power or engine warning lights, have the throttle actuators inspected — £500–£1,000 now is cheaper than the £1,500–£2,500 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare S85 throttle actuator repairs for your M6 →

problems.title: S85 VANOS Wear
problems.affectedModels: M6 E63/E64 all variants (2005–2010) with S85B50 engine.
problems.rootCause: The S85's VANOS system can wear over time, leading to reduced low‑end torque, rough idle, and rattling on start‑up. This is a shared issue with other BMW V8 and V10 engines.
problems.recommendation: On any high‑mileage M6, budget for VANOS inspection as part of routine maintenance — £800–£1,800 now is cheaper than the £8,000–£12,000 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare S85 VANOS repairs for your M6 →

problems.title: SMG III Transmission Issues
problems.rootCause: The 7‑speed SMG III automated manual is a sophisticated gearbox, but it can suffer from hydraulic pump failure, clutch wear, and mechatronics issues — particularly on high‑mileage or tracked cars.
problems.recommendation: On any SMG‑equipped M6, service the transmission every 30k–40k miles — a £200–£500 service is cheap insurance against the £2,500–£4,500 replacement that follows a failure.

problems.title: S85 High Oil Consumption
problems.affectedModels: M6 E63/E64 all variants (2005–2010) with S85B50 engine.
problems.rootCause: The S85 is known for moderate oil consumption — typically 1 litre per 1,000–2,000 miles, which BMW considers normal. However, excessive consumption can indicate valve stem seal wear or piston ring wear.
problems.recommendation: Monitor oil consumption regularly — if it exceeds 1 litre per 1,000 miles, have the valve stem seals inspected.

cta.label: Compare S85 engine prices for your M6 →
